title: Iatrogenic Hypothyroidism in Graves Disease: Don't Chase the Labs Into Thyrotoxicosis
subtitle: A Resident Training Module: over-blockade on methimazole (TSH 85, FT4 twice below normal), why 'stop the drug then restart at a fixed dose' and 'wait for T3/T4 to rise before restarting' are both wrong, the titration vs block-and-replace regimens, why FT4/T3 (not TSH) guide dosing, and the short-interval recheck
authors: Resident/fellowship training module — illustrative teaching case

relatedChapters.title: Severe Hypothyroidism with an Incidental Positive ANA
Graves Disease and Urgent Cancer Surgery

patient.chiefComplaint: Routine follow-up of Graves disease on methimazole; biochemically markedly hypothyroid.

caseDescription: A 47-year-old woman with Graves disease on methimazole 15 mg daily is found, on follow-up, to be markedly hypothyroid: TSH 85 mIU/L, total T3 just below the lower limit of normal, and free T4 about twice below the lower limit. This is over-blockade — iatrogenic hypothyroidism from too much antithyroid drug, not remission. The resident stopped methimazole completely for 5 days, then planned to restart at a fixed 5 mg for 20 days and recheck. A second proposal was to stop the drug for 10 days and restart only once the TSH is suppressed or T3/T4 climb above normal. The teaching question is which is the better, evidence-based approach.
keyFindings: TSH 85 with FT4 twice below normal on methimazole 15 mg = iatrogenic over-blockade, not Graves remission
The high TSH is a trophic drive: with the brake (MMI) removed, FT4/T3 can rebound and overshoot
Resident's plan — 5-day stop then a fixed 5 mg — is an arbitrary dose untethered from thyroid function
The alternative — wait off-drug until T3/T4 are high before restarting — deliberately allows a thyrotoxic relapse
Correct paradigm: reduce/hold the drug now, recheck at a short interval (~2 weeks), and titrate to FT4/T3
TSH lags by weeks–months after prolonged suppression — dose to FT4/T3, not to TSH, early on
finalDiagnosis: Methimazole-induced (iatrogenic) hypothyroidism in Graves disease from excessive antithyroid-drug dosing. Correct management is to drop the methimazole to a low maintenance dose (or briefly hold it) and re-measure thyroid function at a short interval (about 2 weeks), then titrate the dose to keep free T4 and T3 in range — the titration regimen — accepting that TSH recovers last. An alternative is the deliberate block-and-replace approach (continue a fixed methimazole dose and add levothyroxine). Both the 'fixed 5 mg restart' and the 'wait until thyrotoxic, then restart' strategies are wrong: the first is an arbitrary dose, the second engineers an avoidable thyrotoxic relapse with its arrhythmia, bone, and orbitopathy risks.
learningObjectives: Recognise TSH 85 with a low FT4 on methimazole as iatrogenic over-blockade, and distinguish it from true Graves remission.
Explain why dosing to FT4/T3 — not TSH — is correct early on, because TSH lags after prolonged over-suppression.
Critique the 'stop then restart at a fixed dose' plan and the 'wait for thyrotoxicosis before restarting' plan as both wrong, the latter dangerous.
Describe the titration regimen and the block-and-replace alternative, and the evidence/guideline basis for preferring titration.
Set a safe short-interval follow-up (~2 weeks) and titration targets, and counsel the patient on the transitional hypo-/hyperthyroid phase.

items.title: Why TSH 85 with a low FT4 on methimazole means over-treatment, not remission
items.content: Read the pattern mechanistically. On methimazole, the thyroid's hormone output is being pharmacologically suppressed; a free T4 twice below the lower limit with a T3 at the floor shows the gland is over-blocked. The TSH of 85 is the pituitary's response to that low circulating hormone — a powerful trophic drive that has had time to build, which itself tells you the hypothyroidism is not new. This is iatrogenic (drug-induced) hypothyroidism from too much antithyroid drug. It is emphatically not Graves remission: remission means normal hormones off (or on minimal) drug with a normalising TSH-receptor antibody, not a floored FT4 with a sky-high TSH. The action that follows from 'over-blocked' (reduce the drug) is the opposite of what you would do for relapse — so getting this interpretation right is the whole case.
keyPoints: FT4 twice below normal + T3 at floor on MMI = pharmacological over-blockade
TSH 85 is a trophic drive that took time to build — not acute, not remission
Over-blockade ⇒ reduce the drug; the opposite action to treating a relapse

items.title: Why the high TSH sets up a rebound overshoot when the brake comes off
items.content: Methimazole inhibits thyroid peroxidase and so blocks new hormone synthesis, but the underlying Graves disease — driven by stimulating TSH-receptor antibodies — is still active. When you remove the methimazole entirely, two stimulators now act on an unblocked gland: the persistent TSH-receptor antibody and the markedly elevated TSH (85). The gland can therefore rebound briskly, and because there is a ~6–7 day lag from the long half-life of T4 and the time to resynthesise hormone, the overshoot may not appear for several days — and can then climb past normal into thyrotoxicosis. This is exactly why a 'complete stop and watch' with a distant recheck is risky: the rebound can outrun the follow-up.
keyPoints: MMI blocks synthesis but the TRAb-driven Graves process continues
Removing MMI leaves gland exposed to both TRAb and a very high TSH ⇒ brisk rebound
T4's long half-life delays the overshoot — a distant recheck can miss it

items.title: Why 'stop for 5 days then restart at a fixed 5 mg for 20 days' is the wrong plan
items.content: There are two errors. First, a complete 5-day stop is a blunt, arbitrary washout: stopping the drug in an over-blocked patient is reasonable in principle, but a fixed off-period chosen by the calendar rather than by thyroid function is not control. Second, restarting at a fixed 5 mg untethered from any measured hormone level is the deeper mistake — it treats dosing as a fixed recipe rather than a titration. 5 mg may be too much for a gland still recovering from prolonged suppression (re-inducing hypothyroidism) and too little if the TRAb-driven gland surges (failing to control a relapse). Choosing a dose without a current FT4/T3 to anchor it is the worst of both worlds, and rechecking only at 20 days risks missing a fast rebound in between.
keyPoints: A calendar-based fixed stop is not the same as controlled, function-guided dosing
Fixed 5 mg with no current FT4/T3 = arbitrary: may be too much OR too little
A 20-day-only recheck can miss a brisk interim rebound

items.title: Why 'wait off-drug until T3/T4 rise above normal, then restart' is dangerous
items.content: This proposal — hold methimazole for 10 days and restart only once TSH is suppressed or T3/T4 are frankly high — formalises a 'chase the labs' error: it deliberately waits for the patient to become thyrotoxic before acting. That guarantees an interval of untreated hyperthyroidism, with its real harms: atrial fibrillation and other arrhythmias, accelerated bone loss, weight loss and catabolism, and — important in Graves — worsening of thyroid eye disease, which is aggravated by swings in thyroid status. The whole point of antithyroid therapy is to prevent end-organ damage from thyrotoxicosis, not to allow it and then react. Proactive, pre-emptive titration beats reactive rescue.
keyPoints: Restarting only after T3/T4 are high = engineering an avoidable thyrotoxic relapse
Untreated hyperthyroidism risks AF, bone loss, catabolism and worse orbitopathy
ATD therapy is meant to prevent thyrotoxic end-organ damage, not chase it

items.title: How to manage the over-blocked patient today: reduce, don't yo-yo
items.content: The cleaner move than a full stop is to cut the methimazole to a low maintenance dose now (commonly 2.5–5 mg/day) — or, if you prefer a brief hold, hold for only a few days and then resume low-dose — and to plan a short-interval recheck rather than a distant one. Reducing rather than abruptly stopping keeps some control of the underlying Graves activity while letting the over-suppressed hormones recover, and avoids the hypo→hyper 'yo-yo'. If the patient is symptomatically hypothyroid in the interim, a short course of levothyroxine can be added (this is effectively a temporary block-and-replace) rather than abandoning the antithyroid drug altogether. The key change from the resident's plan is that the next dose decision is made against a fresh FT4/T3, not the calendar.
keyPoints: Drop MMI to low maintenance (≈2.5–5 mg) rather than a blunt full stop
Reducing keeps Graves control while hormones recover — avoids the yo-yo
Add short-course levothyroxine if symptomatically hypothyroid (temporary block-and-replace)

items.title: Why dose to free T4 and T3 — not TSH — in the early phase
items.content: After prolonged over-suppression the pituitary thyrotrophs are slow to recalibrate, so TSH lags behind the thyroid hormones by weeks to months. If you dose to 'normalise the TSH', the TSH of 85 will tempt you to push hormone up (or pull antithyroid drug right back) just as FT4/T3 are climbing — and you will overshoot into thyrotoxicosis. Early on, free T4 and total T3 are the steering instruments: titrate the methimazole to keep them in range and accept that TSH will remain high (then low) and will be the last parameter to normalise. Only once the axis re-equilibrates does TSH become a reliable target.
keyPoints: TSH lags FT4/T3 by weeks–months after prolonged suppression
Dosing to normalise a lagging TSH drives overshoot
Titrate to FT4/T3 early; let TSH be the last to recover

items.title: How soon to recheck — a short interval, not 20 days alone
items.content: Because a rebound can develop within days of dropping the drug, the recheck should be short-interval — about 2 weeks — to catch the gland's direction before it overshoots, with the dose then set against that result. A single distant 20-day check is too coarse for a patient who has just had a major dose change from an over-blocked baseline. Practically: reduce the dose now, recheck FT4/T3 (and TSH) at ~2 weeks, and re-titrate; once stable, intervals lengthen to 4–6 weeks and then every few months. Frequent early sampling is what makes titration safe.
keyPoints: Recheck FT4/T3 (+TSH) at ~2 weeks after a major dose change
A lone 20-day check is too coarse from an over-blocked baseline
Lengthen intervals to 4–6 weeks, then months, once stable

items.title: How the titration regimen works (and why it is first-line)
items.content: In the titration ('dose-to-effect') regimen you give the lowest antithyroid-drug dose that keeps the patient euthyroid: start higher to gain control, then taper to a maintenance dose (often 2.5–10 mg methimazole) guided by FT4/T3. It is the preferred first-line regimen in major guidance because it exposes the patient to less drug and fewer dose-related adverse effects than block-and-replace, while achieving comparable control. The present patient is a textbook titration problem: she overshot into hypothyroidism on a fixed 15 mg because the dose was not tapered as she responded — the fix is to taper now and steer by hormone levels.
keyPoints: Titration = lowest ATD dose that keeps the patient euthyroid, steered by FT4/T3
Preferred first-line: less total drug, fewer dose-related adverse effects
Her over-blockade is a failure to taper a fixed dose as she responded

items.title: How block-and-replace differs, and when it is reasonable
items.content: In block-and-replace you keep a fixed, fully blocking methimazole dose and add levothyroxine to replace what you have blocked, holding the patient euthyroid from both sides. Its advantages are more stable control and fewer thyroid-function tests; its drawback is a higher antithyroid-drug dose and therefore more dose-related side-effects, which is why it is not first-line for most patients. It is genuinely useful when thyroid levels are hard to stabilise or swing widely, and a short, temporary version (adding levothyroxine while continuing low-dose methimazole) is a sensible bridge for a patient like this who has just been over-blocked and is symptomatically hypothyroid. It should be avoided in pregnancy (the levothyroxine crosses the placenta poorly relative to the antithyroid drug, risking fetal hypothyroidism).
keyPoints: Block-and-replace = full-blocking MMI + levothyroxine; more stable, fewer tests
Costs more drug and more side-effects ⇒ not first-line for most
Avoid in pregnancy; a brief version can bridge an over-blocked, symptomatic patient

items.title: Why this visit is also the moment to re-counsel on methimazole safety
items.content: Any methimazole encounter should reinforce the two serious adverse effects, because patients quickly forget them. Agranulocytosis is rare but dangerous: tell her to stop the drug and get an urgent full blood count if she develops fever or a severe sore throat, rather than waiting it out — routine serial counts do not reliably catch it. Hepatotoxicity (methimazole tends to cause cholestatic injury; propylthiouracil causes hepatocellular injury) warrants stopping and testing if she develops jaundice, dark urine, pale stools or right-upper-quadrant pain. Both are dose-related in part, which is a further reason the titration regimen — using the least drug needed — is preferred. None of this changes because she is currently hypothyroid; it is part of safe ATD stewardship at every visit.
keyPoints: Agranulocytosis: stop + urgent FBC for fever/severe sore throat (serial counts don't catch it)
Hepatotoxicity: stop + LFTs for jaundice/dark urine/RUQ pain
Adverse effects are partly dose-related — another argument for titration

items.title: Why recurrent dose instability should prompt the definitive-therapy conversation
items.content: A single episode of over-blockade is managed by titration. But repeated difficulty holding a steady euthyroid state, frequent relapses, poor adherence, or persistently high TSH-receptor antibody after a course of antithyroid drug should prompt a discussion of definitive therapy — radioactive iodine or total thyroidectomy — each followed by lifelong levothyroxine. The choice is individualised: radioactive iodine is avoided in pregnancy/breastfeeding and used cautiously in active, moderate-to-severe thyroid eye disease (it can worsen orbitopathy, where surgery or steroid cover is preferred); surgery gives the fastest definitive control and is favoured for large goitres, suspicious nodules, or when rapid resolution is needed. The teaching point: antithyroid drugs are not the only path, and chronic instability is a cue to revisit the plan.
keyPoints: Manage this episode by titration; flag recurrent instability for definitive therapy
RAI vs surgery, each followed by lifelong levothyroxine — individualised choice
RAI can worsen active orbitopathy; surgery favoured for large goitre/suspicious nodule

items.title: How to act on an over-blocked Graves patient — a one-page rule
items.content: Practical algorithm for high TSH + low FT4 on an antithyroid drug: (1) Name it — iatrogenic over-blockade, not remission. (2) Reduce the antithyroid drug to a low maintenance dose now (≈2.5–5 mg methimazole); brief hold is acceptable but avoid an open-ended stop. (3) Add short-course levothyroxine only if symptomatically hypothyroid (temporary block-and-replace). (4) Steer by free T4 and T3, not TSH — TSH lags. (5) Recheck at a short interval (~2 weeks), then re-titrate; lengthen intervals once stable. (6) Never wait for frank thyrotoxicosis before acting. (7) Re-counsel on agranulocytosis and hepatotoxicity warning signs. (8) If instability recurs, discuss definitive therapy.
keyPoints: Reduce drug now; steer by FT4/T3; recheck ~2 weeks; titrate
Don't wait for thyrotoxicosis; don't pick a fixed dose blind
Re-counsel on ATD safety; flag recurrent instability for definitive therapy

items.question: Why is it wrong to use the TSH of 85 as the target to dose against in this patient?
items.answer: Because TSH is a lagging indicator here. After prolonged over-suppression of thyroid hormone, the pituitary thyrotrophs take weeks to months to recalibrate, so the TSH reflects where the patient was, not where she is heading. If you treat the TSH of 85 as the thing to fix, you will push thyroid hormone up (or pull the antithyroid drug right off) precisely as the unblocked, TRAb- and TSH-stimulated gland is already rebounding — and you will overshoot into thyrotoxicosis. In the early phase the free T4 and total T3 move first and are the correct steering instruments; the methimazole dose is titrated to hold them in range, and the high TSH is expected to be the last parameter to normalise.
keyPoints: TSH lags FT4/T3 by weeks–months after prolonged over-suppression
Dosing to a lagging TSH drives overshoot into thyrotoxicosis
Steer by FT4/T3 early; TSH normalises last

items.question: How should this patient actually be managed, step by step?
items.answer: (1) Name the problem: iatrogenic over-blockade (TSH 85, FT4 twice below normal on MMI 15 mg), not remission. (2) Reduce methimazole to a low maintenance dose now (≈2.5–5 mg/day) rather than an open-ended stop — a brief few-day hold is acceptable but the next dose must be function-guided. (3) If she is symptomatically hypothyroid, add a short course of levothyroxine (temporary block-and-replace) instead of abandoning the antithyroid drug. (4) Recheck free T4 and total T3 (and TSH) at a short interval — about 2 weeks — because a rebound can develop within days. (5) Re-titrate to keep FT4/T3 in range, dosing to those rather than to the lagging TSH; lengthen intervals to 4–6 weeks once stable. (6) Re-counsel on agranulocytosis and hepatotoxicity warning signs. (7) If instability recurs, discuss definitive therapy (radioactive iodine or surgery, each with lifelong levothyroxine).
keyPoints: Reduce MMI to low maintenance now; add short LT4 only if symptomatic
Recheck FT4/T3 at ~2 weeks; titrate to FT4/T3, not TSH
Re-counsel on ATD safety; consider definitive therapy if instability recurs

items.stem: A 47-year-old woman with Graves disease on methimazole 15 mg daily has TSH 85 mIU/L, total T3 just below normal and free T4 twice below normal. What does this pattern represent?
options: Iatrogenic hypothyroidism from antithyroid-drug over-blockade
Spontaneous remission of Graves disease
Development of central (secondary) hypothyroidism
Assay interference requiring no action
items.explanation: A floored FT4 and T3 with a markedly elevated TSH on a fixed methimazole dose is over-blockade — too much antithyroid drug. Remission would show normal hormones with a normalising TSH (and falling TRAb), not a high TSH with low FT4. Central hypothyroidism would give a low/inappropriately-normal TSH, and the picture is too coherent to be assay artefact.

items.stem: Which is the best management of this over-blocked patient?
options: Reduce methimazole to a low maintenance dose and recheck FT4/T3 at about 2 weeks, then titrate
Continue methimazole 15 mg and add levothyroxine to normalise the TSH quickly
Stop methimazole and restart only once T3/T4 rise above the normal range
Stop methimazole for 5 days then restart a fixed 5 mg for 20 days regardless of levels
items.explanation: The titration approach — drop the drug to a low maintenance dose now and re-measure at a short interval, dosing to FT4/T3 — is correct and safe. Continuing 15 mg keeps her over-blocked; waiting for T3/T4 to rise engineers a thyrotoxic relapse; and a fixed 5 mg untethered from current levels is arbitrary.

items.stem: In the first weeks after correcting over-blockade in Graves disease, which parameter should primarily guide methimazole dosing?
options: Free T4 and total T3
TSH
TSH-receptor antibody titre
Thyroid uptake scan
items.explanation: TSH lags by weeks to months after prolonged over-suppression, so it is unreliable as an early target. Free T4 and total T3 move first and steer titration. TRAb informs prognosis/relapse risk, not day-to-day dosing, and an uptake scan has no role here.

items.stem: Why is the proposal to stop methimazole and restart only when T3/T4 are frankly high unsafe?
options: It deliberately allows an interval of untreated thyrotoxicosis with arrhythmia, bone-loss and orbitopathy risk
It uses too low a maintenance dose of antithyroid drug
It will inevitably cause permanent hypothyroidism
It risks immediate agranulocytosis from drug withdrawal
items.explanation: Restarting only after the patient is biochemically hyperthyroid is a 'chase the labs' error: it guarantees a period of untreated thyrotoxicosis (atrial fibrillation, bone loss, catabolism, worsening thyroid eye disease). Antithyroid therapy exists to prevent this, not to react to it.

items.stem: Compared with block-and-replace, the titration regimen for Graves disease is generally preferred because it:
options: Uses less total antithyroid drug and causes fewer dose-related adverse effects
Achieves remission in nearly all patients
Eliminates the need for any thyroid-function monitoring
Avoids agranulocytosis entirely
items.explanation: Titration gives the lowest dose that keeps the patient euthyroid, exposing them to less drug and fewer dose-related side-effects than block-and-replace, with comparable control — hence first-line in major guidance. It does not change remission rates dramatically, still requires monitoring, and does not abolish idiosyncratic agranulocytosis.

items.stem: After methimazole is stopped completely in this patient, why might thyrotoxicosis not appear for several days?
options: T4's long (~7-day) half-life and the time to resynthesise hormone delay the rise
TSH-receptor antibodies disappear immediately when the drug stops
The pituitary suppresses the gland for a week after drug withdrawal
Methimazole permanently destroys thyroid follicular cells
items.explanation: Once the synthesis block is removed, the gland (still driven by TRAb and a high TSH) resumes hormone production, but the long half-life of T4 and the lag to resynthesise hormone mean the overshoot is delayed by several days — which is why a distant recheck can miss it. TRAb persists, the pituitary is driving (not suppressing), and methimazole does not destroy the gland.

items.statement: Early after correcting over-blockade, the methimazole dose should be titrated to normalise the TSH as quickly as possible.
items.explanation: False. TSH lags by weeks to months after prolonged over-suppression. Dosing to normalise a lagging TSH drives an overshoot into thyrotoxicosis. Titrate to free T4 and T3 early; let TSH recover last.

items.statement: A markedly elevated TSH with a low free T4 on a fixed antithyroid-drug dose calls for reducing the drug, not increasing it.
items.explanation: True. That pattern is over-blockade — too much antithyroid drug — so the dose should be reduced (to a low maintenance dose) and the patient re-measured at a short interval, with short-course levothyroxine added only if symptomatically hypothyroid.

items.assertion: Restarting methimazole at a fixed 5 mg without a current free T4/T3 is an unreliable plan.
items.reason: Antithyroid-drug therapy for Graves disease should be titrated to thyroid hormone levels rather than given as a fixed recipe.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion. Because Graves disease is managed by titrating the dose to keep the patient euthyroid, a fixed dose chosen without a current FT4/T3 is untethered from the patient's actual thyroid status — it may be too much for a recovering gland or too little for a relapsing one. Hence option 0.

items.assertion: Waiting until T3 and T4 rise above normal before restarting an antithyroid drug is a safe monitoring strategy in Graves disease.
items.reason: Brief, controlled hyperthyroidism carries no meaningful cardiovascular or skeletal risk.
items.explanation: Both statements are false. Waiting for frank thyrotoxicosis before acting deliberately allows untreated hyperthyroidism, and even short periods carry real risks — atrial fibrillation and other arrhythmias, accelerated bone loss, catabolism, and worsening of thyroid eye disease. Hence option 4.

items.question: State the two specific errors in the resident's 'stop for 5 days, then fixed 5 mg for 20 days' plan.
items.answer: (1) The restart dose is arbitrary — a fixed 5 mg chosen without a current free T4/T3 is untethered from the patient's actual thyroid status, and may be too much for a recovering over-suppressed gland or too little for a relapsing TRAb-driven one; Graves disease should be titrated to hormone levels, not dosed by recipe. (2) The monitoring interval is too coarse — a rebound can develop within days of removing the drug, so a single check at 20 days can miss an interim overshoot. The fix is to reduce to a low maintenance dose and recheck FT4/T3 at about 2 weeks, then titrate.

items.question: Contrast the titration and block-and-replace regimens and say which is first-line and why.
items.answer: Titration ('dose-to-effect') gives the lowest antithyroid-drug dose that keeps the patient euthyroid, guided by free T4/T3. Block-and-replace keeps a fixed, fully blocking antithyroid-drug dose and adds levothyroxine to replace what is blocked, giving more stable control with fewer thyroid-function tests but a higher drug dose. Titration is first-line for most patients because it uses less total drug and causes fewer dose-related adverse effects while achieving comparable control. Block-and-replace is reserved for hard-to-stabilise/fluctuating cases and is avoided in pregnancy (risk of fetal hypothyroidism).

items.question: What two serious methimazole adverse effects must the patient be re-counselled about, and what should she do?
items.answer: (1) Agranulocytosis — rare but dangerous: stop the drug and get an urgent full blood count if she develops fever or a severe sore throat, rather than waiting (routine serial counts do not reliably catch it). (2) Hepatotoxicity (methimazole typically causes cholestatic injury): stop the drug and check liver function if she develops jaundice, dark urine, pale stools or right-upper-quadrant pain. Both are partly dose-related, a further reason to use the lowest effective (titrated) dose.

items.assertion: Using the TSH of 85 as the target to dose against in this patient is incorrect.
items.reason: Because TSH is a lagging indicator that reflects the patient's prior thyroid status rather than current thyroid function, and dosing based on it risks overshooting into thyrotoxicosis.
items.explanation: TSH lags behind free T4 and total T3 by weeks–months after prolonged over-suppression; steering by FT4/T3 prevents thyrotoxicosis overshoot.

items.stem: Which antithyroid drug regimen is first-line for most patients with hyperthyroidism, and why?
options: A. Titration, as it uses lower total drug doses and causes fewer adverse effects while maintaining euthyroidism
B. Block-and-replace, as it provides more stable thyroid function control with fewer laboratory tests
C. Block-and-replace, as it avoids the risk of fetal hypothyroidism in pregnancy
D. Titration, as it eliminates the need for levothyroxine supplementation
items.explanation: Titration (dose-to-effect) is first-line because it achieves euthyroidism with lower drug doses, minimizing adverse effects, while block-and-replace (fixed antithyroid drug + levothyroxine) is reserved for unstable cases and avoided in pregnancy due to fetal hypothyroidism risk.

items.stem: Which two serious adverse effects of methimazole require immediate patient action if symptoms develop?
options: A. Agranulocytosis and hepatotoxicity — stop the drug and seek urgent blood tests for fever/throat pain or jaundice symptoms
B. Hypothyroidism and rash — monitor symptoms and adjust dose if needed
C. Goiter and hypertension — discontinue drug and consult a specialist
D. Nephrotoxicity and arrhythmia — stop drug and perform ECG and renal function tests
items.explanation: Agranulocytosis (leukopenia) and hepatotoxicity are life-threatening methimazole complications requiring immediate discontinuation and specific investigations as described. Other options are either less severe or not typically associated with methimazole.

Okay, let's tackle this query. The user wants to convert a Q&A into an objective exam question for Harrison's. The original question is about two serious adverse effects of methimazole and what the patient should do.
